---
title: "Further Pre-Processing"
author: "Rohan"
date: "2025-08-03"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r}
library(purrr)
library(GGally)
library(tidyr)
library(sampling)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(corrplot)
library(fastDummies)
library(factoextra)
library(cluster)
library(scales)
library(dlookr)
set.seed(123)
```

Defining global target variable, dataset_name and variables that will not go through encoding for the sake of reducing dimentionality
```{r}

# 1) Path to your CSV file:
dataset_path    <- "../Datasets/Merged_Dataset.csv"

# 2) Name of the column you want to predict (the “target”):
target_variable <- "Alzheimer.s.Diagnosis"

# 3) Define your output folder relative to current wd
outdir <- "../All Reports/2. Pre-processing Reports"
if (!dir.exists(outdir)) dir.create(outdir, recursive = TRUE)
```


Creating user defined functions to Load Data

```{r}
# Function to load a CSV file into a data.frame
load_data <- function(path) {
  read.csv(
    path,
    stringsAsFactors = TRUE,    # keep character columns as characters
    na.strings      = c("", "NA", " ")  # treat these as missing
  )
}

# Load and inspect using the configured path
df <- load_data(dataset_path)
```


```{r}
# Function to save a dlookr diagnose report with an “initial” prefix
save_diagnose_report <- function(df, name, prefix = "Initial_") {
  report    <- df %>% diagnose()
  fname     <- paste0(prefix, "_", name, "_diagnose_report.csv")
  file_path <- file.path(outdir, fname)
  write.csv(report, file_path, row.names = FALSE)
  message("Saved report to ", file_path)
}

# Function to save summary report
save_summary_csv <- function(df, name, prefix = "Initial_") {
  
  # 1) numeric summaries: min, Q1, median, mean, Q3, max
  num_vars  <- names(df)[sapply(df, is.numeric)]
  num_stats <- df %>%
    summarise(across(all_of(num_vars),
                     list(
                       min    = ~ min(.x,   na.rm = TRUE),
                       Q1     = ~ quantile(.x, .25, na.rm = TRUE),
                       median = ~ median(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
                       mean   = ~ mean(.x,   na.rm = TRUE),
                       Q3     = ~ quantile(.x, .75, na.rm = TRUE),
                       max    = ~ max(.x,   na.rm = TRUE)
                     ),
                     .names = "{col}_{fn}"
    )) %>%
    pivot_longer(
      cols      = everything(),
      names_to  = c("variable", "stat"),
      names_sep = "_"
    ) %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = stat, values_from = value)
  
  # 2) factor summaries: number of levels + labels
  fac_vars  <- names(df)[sapply(df, is.factor)]
  fac_stats <- tibble(
    variable     = fac_vars,
    n_categories = map_int(fac_vars, ~ nlevels(df[[.x]])),
    categories   = map_chr(fac_vars, ~ paste(levels(df[[.x]]), collapse = ", "))
  )
  
  # 3) join into one table
  summary_tbl <- full_join(num_stats, fac_stats, by = "variable")
  
  # 4) write out
  fname <- paste0(prefix, "_", name, "_summary.csv")
  fpath <- file.path(outdir, fname)
  write.csv(summary_tbl, fpath, row.names = FALSE)
  message("Saved summary CSV to ", fpath)
}

# Save dlookr diagnose report for the cleaned data
save_diagnose_report(df, name = "dataset",)

# Save summary CSV for the cleaned data
save_summary_csv(df, name = "dataset")
```


```{r}
# Compute mode (works for vectors)
get_mode <- function(v) {
  uniqv <- unique(na.omit(v))
  uniqv[which.max(tabulate(match(v, uniqv)))]
}

# Clean missing values & coerce numeric-like text to numeric
clean_missing_values <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    mutate(across(
      where(is.character),
      ~{
        clean <- gsub("[^0-9\\.-]", "", .)         # strip non-numeric chars
        clean[clean == ""] <- NA                   # empty → NA
        num <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(clean)) # try numeric
        if (any(!is.na(num))) num else .           # keep numeric if any
      }
    )) %>%
    mutate(across(
      where(is.numeric),
      ~replace(., is.na(.), median(., na.rm = TRUE))  # NAs → median
    )) %>%
    mutate(across(
      where(~ is.factor(.) || is.character(.)),      # factor/char cols
      ~replace(., is.na(.), get_mode(.))              # NAs → mode
    ))
}

# Replace outliers (beyond the boxplot “whiskers”) with median
replace_outliers_with_median <- function(df) {
  num_cols <- names(df)[sapply(df, is.numeric)]
  for (col in num_cols) {
    out_vals <- boxplot(df[[col]], plot = FALSE)$out
    df[[col]][df[[col]] %in% out_vals] <- NA
    df[[col]][is.na(df[[col]])]       <- median(df[[col]], na.rm = TRUE)
  }
  df
}

# Convert any remaining character columns into factors
convert_char_to_factor <- function(df) {
  df %>% mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor))
}

# ─── EXECUTE CLEANING PIPELINE ────────────────────────────────────────────────
df <- df %>%
  clean_missing_values() %>%
  replace_outliers_with_median() %>%
  convert_char_to_factor()

# ─── SAVE CLEANING REPORTS USING EXISTING HELPERS ──────────────────────────────

# Save dlookr diagnose report for the cleaned data
save_diagnose_report(df, name = "dataset", prefix = "Cleaned")

# Save summary CSV for the cleaned data
save_summary_csv(df, name = "dataset", prefix = "Cleaned")

```

Save plot function
```{r}
# ─── Helper to save any ggplot to JPG, using an existing `outdir` ───────────────
save_plot_jpg <- function(plot_obj, filename, dpi = 300) {
  
  # 1) Build the output path
  file_path <- file.path(outdir, paste0(filename, ".jpg"))
  
  # 2) Save the plot; width/height are inferred automatically
  ggplot2::ggsave(
    filename = file_path,
    plot     = plot_obj,
    device   = "jpeg",
    dpi      = dpi
  )
}
```


```{r}
# ─── Combined boxplots without overlaid points ─────────────────────────────────
plot_boxplots_by_target <- function(df, target_col) {
  # ensure the target is a factor
  df[[target_col]] <- as.factor(df[[target_col]])
  
  # identify numeric columns (excluding the target)
  num_vars <- setdiff(names(df)[sapply(df, is.numeric)], target_col)
  
  # pivot to long for faceting
  plot_df <- df %>%
    dplyr::select(all_of(c(target_col, num_vars))) %>%
    tidyr::pivot_longer(
      cols      = all_of(num_vars),
      names_to  = "variable",
      values_to = "value"
    )
  
  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = .data[[target_col]], y = value)) +
    geom_boxplot(
      width         = 0.6,
      outlier.shape = NA,
      fill          = "#8DA0CB",
      color         = "#37474F",
      alpha         = 0.8
    ) +
    facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) +
    theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor    = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major    = element_line(color = "grey90"),
      strip.background    = element_rect(fill = "grey85", color = NA),
      strip.text          = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9),
      axis.text.x         = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
      plot.title          = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold")
    ) +
    labs(
      title = paste("Numeric Features by", target_col),
      x     = target_col,
      y     = "Value"
    )
}

# Usage
plot_boxplots_by_target(df, target_variable)

# Saving the plot
bp <- plot_boxplots_by_target(df, target_variable)
save_plot_jpg(bp, "Boxplots_by_target")
```


```{r}
# Combined histograms with overlaid density in one multi‐facet plot
plot_numeric_distributions <- function(df) {
  # 1) Identify all numeric columns
  num_vars <- names(df)[sapply(df, is.numeric)]
  
  # 2) Pivot to long for faceting
  plot_df <- df %>%
    dplyr::select(all_of(num_vars)) %>%
    tidyr::pivot_longer(
      cols      = everything(),
      names_to  = "variable",
      values_to = "value"
    )
  
  # 3) Single ggplot with histogram + density facets
  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = value)) +
    geom_histogram(
      aes(y = ..density..),
      bins  = 30,
      fill  = "steelblue",
      color = "white",
      alpha = 0.7
    ) +
    geom_density(
      color = "darkred",
      size  = 0.8
    ) +
    facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 4) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor    = element_blank(),
      strip.background    = element_rect(fill = "grey90", color = NA),
      strip.text          = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9),
      axis.text.x         = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
      plot.title          = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold")
    ) +
    labs(
      title = "Numeric Distributions with Density Curves",
      x     = "Value",
      y     = "Density"
    )
}

# Usage: this will draw all numeric distributions in one plot
plot_numeric_distributions(df)

# Saving the plot
hp <- plot_numeric_distributions(df)
save_plot_jpg(hp, "Histogram")

```


```{r}
#Improved combined barplots for all categorical features vs target
plot_categorical_vs_target_facet <- function(df, target_col,
                                             palette = c("#4C72B0", "#55A868"),
                                             ncol    = NULL) {
  # 1) Identify all categorical columns except the target
  cat_cols <- setdiff(names(df)[sapply(df, is.factor)], target_col)
  n_vars   <- length(cat_cols)
  
  # 2) Decide on number of columns if not provided
  if (is.null(ncol)) {
    ncol <- ceiling(sqrt(n_vars))  # roughly square layout
  }
  
  # 3) Pivot to long for faceting
  df_long <- df %>%
    dplyr::select(all_of(c(target_col, cat_cols))) %>%
    tidyr::pivot_longer(
      cols      = all_of(cat_cols),
      names_to  = "variable",
      values_to = "level"
    )
  
  # 4) Build the faceted barplot
  p <- ggplot(df_long, aes(x = level, fill = .data[[target_col]])) +
    geom_bar(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = palette, name = target_col) +
    facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_x", ncol = ncol) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(
      axis.text.x        = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 7),
      strip.text         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 8),
      panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
      plot.title         = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      panel.spacing      = unit(0.5, "lines")
    ) +
    labs(
      title = paste("Counts of categorical features by", target_col),
      x     = NULL,
      y     = "Count"
    )
  
  print(p)
  invisible(p)
}

#Generate & save the categorical plot

p_cat <- plot_categorical_vs_target_facet(df, target_variable)
save_plot_jpg(p_cat, "Categorical_vs_Target")
```

```{r}
# Combined proportional barplots for all categorical features by target
plot_categorical_prop_by_target <- function(df, target_col,
                                            palette = c("#4C72B0", "#55A868"),
                                            ncol    = NULL) {
  # 1) Identify all categorical columns except the target
  cat_cols <- setdiff(names(df)[sapply(df, is.factor)], target_col)
  n_vars   <- length(cat_cols)
  
  # 2) Decide on number of columns for facets if not provided
  if (is.null(ncol)) {
    ncol <- ceiling(sqrt(n_vars))
  }
  
  # 3) Pivot to long format
  df_long <- df %>%
    dplyr::select(all_of(c(target_col, cat_cols))) %>%
    tidyr::pivot_longer(
      cols      = all_of(cat_cols),
      names_to  = "variable",
      values_to = "level"
    )
  
  # 4) Build the faceted proportional barplot
  p <- ggplot(df_long, aes(x = level, fill = .data[[target_col]])) +
    geom_bar(position = "fill", width = 0.7) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = palette, name = target_col) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
    facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_x", ncol = ncol) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(
      strip.text          = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9),
      axis.text.x         = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
      panel.grid.minor    = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x  = element_blank(),
      plot.title          = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold")
    ) +
    labs(
      title = paste("Proportion of categorical features by", target_col),
      x     = NULL,
      y     = "Proportion"
    )
  
  print(p)
  invisible(p)
}

#Generate & save the plot
p_cat_prop <- plot_categorical_prop_by_target(df, target_variable)
save_plot_jpg(p_cat_prop, "Categorical_prop_by_Target")

```

```{r}
# Generalized Chi-squared tests helper
run_and_save_chi2_tests <- function(df, target_col, filename = "Chi_squared_results") {
  # 1) identify all factor columns except the target
  cat_cols <- setdiff(names(df)[sapply(df, is.factor)], target_col)
  
  # 2) initialize results list
  results <- vector("list", length(cat_cols))
  names(results) <- cat_cols
  
  # 3) loop through each categorical variable
  for (col in cat_cols) {
    tbl <- table(df[[col]], df[[target_col]])
    if (nrow(tbl) > 1 && ncol(tbl) > 1) {
      test <- suppressWarnings(chisq.test(tbl))
      results[[col]] <- data.frame(
        variable    = col,
        statistic   = round(test$statistic, 3),
        df          = test$parameter,
        p_value     = round(test$p.value, 4),
        significant = ifelse(test$p.value < 0.05, "yes", "no"),
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      )
    } else {
      results[[col]] <- data.frame(
        variable    = col,
        statistic   = NA,
        df          = NA,
        p_value     = NA,
        significant = "skipped",
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      )
    }
  }
  
  # 4) combine into one data.frame
  res_df <- do.call(rbind, results)
  
  # 5) print to console
  print(res_df)
  
  # 6) save to CSV in the predefined outdir
  write.csv(
    res_df,
    file = file.path(outdir, paste0(filename, ".csv")),
    row.names = FALSE
  )
  message("Saved chi-squared test results to: ", file.path(outdir, paste0(filename, ".csv")))
  
  invisible(res_df)
}

#Usage
# Run tests on all categorical variables vs. the target, save results
run_and_save_chi2_tests(df, target_variable)

```

```{r}
# Barplot of target class counts with labels
plot_target_distribution_counts <- function(df, target_col,
                                            palette = c("#4C72B0", "#55A868")) {
  # 1) Compute counts by class
  df_counts <- df %>%
    dplyr::count(!!rlang::sym(target_col)) %>%
    dplyr::rename(class = !!rlang::sym(target_col), n = n)
  
  # 2) Build the barplot with count labels
  p <- ggplot2::ggplot(df_counts, ggplot2::aes(x = class, y = n, fill = class)) +
    ggplot2::geom_col(width = 0.6) +
    ggplot2::geom_text(
      ggplot2::aes(label = n),
      vjust = -0.5,
      size  = 3.5
    ) +
    # use "none" instead of FALSE to suppress the legend
    ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = palette, guide = "none") +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    ggplot2::theme(
      axis.text.x      = ggplot2::element_text(size = 10),
      panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank(),
      plot.title       = ggplot2::element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold")
    ) +
    ggplot2::labs(
      title = paste("Class Distribution of", target_col),
      x     = target_col,
      y     = "Count"
    )
  
  print(p)
  invisible(p)
}

#Generate & save the plot
p_target <- plot_target_distribution_counts(df, target_variable)
save_plot_jpg(p_target, "Target_distribution_plot_with_counts")
```

```{r}
# Generalized helper to compute, display, and save correlation matrix as CSV
save_correlation_matrix_csv <- function(df,
                                        exclude_cols = NULL,
                                        filename     = "Correlation_matrix_values") {
  # 1) ensure global outdir exists
  if (!exists("outdir")) stop("Please define `outdir` before calling this function")
  if (!dir.exists(outdir)) dir.create(outdir, recursive = TRUE)
  
  # 2) select only numeric columns, drop exclusions
  num_df <- df %>% dplyr::select(where(is.numeric))
  if (!is.null(exclude_cols)) {
    num_df <- num_df %>% dplyr::select(-dplyr::any_of(exclude_cols))
  }
  
  # 3) compute correlation matrix
  cm <- cor(num_df, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
  
  # 4) print rounded matrix to console
  print(round(cm, 3))
  
  # 5) save full matrix as CSV
  file_path <- file.path(outdir, paste0(filename, ".csv"))
  write.csv(cm, file = file_path, row.names = TRUE)
  
  message("Saved correlation matrix values to: ", file_path)
}

#Usage
# Exclude target variable and any ID columns from correlation
save_correlation_matrix_csv(
  df,
  exclude_cols = c(target_variable, "ID"),
  filename     = "Correlation_matrix"
)

```


```{r}
# Generalized correlation matrix function (displays & saves)
plot_and_save_correlation_matrix <- function(df,
                                             exclude_cols = NULL,
                                             filename     = "Correlation_matrix",
                                             method       = "color",
                                             order        = "hclust",
                                             tl.cex       = 0.7,
                                             cl.cex       = 0.7,
                                             width        = 800,
                                             height       = 800,
                                             res          = 150) {
  
  # 1) select numeric columns, drop exclusions
  num_df <- df %>% dplyr::select(where(is.numeric))
  if (!is.null(exclude_cols)) {
    num_df <- num_df %>% dplyr::select(-dplyr::any_of(exclude_cols))
  }
  
  # 2) compute correlation matrix
  cm <- cor(num_df, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
  
  # 3) display inline
  corrplot::corrplot(
    cm,
    method = method,
    order  = order,
    tl.cex = tl.cex,
    cl.cex = cl.cex,
    title  = "Correlation Matrix",
    mar    = c(0,0,1,0)
  )
  
  # 4) save to JPG
  file_path <- file.path(outdir, paste0(filename, ".jpg"))
  jpeg(filename = file_path, width = width, height = height, res = res)
  corrplot::corrplot(
    cm,
    method = method,
    order  = order,
    tl.cex = tl.cex,
    cl.cex = cl.cex,
    title  = "Correlation Matrix",
    mar    = c(0,0,1,0)
  )
  dev.off()
  
  message("Saved correlation matrix to: ", file_path)
}

#Usage
plot_and_save_correlation_matrix(
  df,
  exclude_cols = c(target_variable, "ID")
)

```
